title: Abundance of Fungal Hyphae in Seawater by Epifluorescence Microscopy
author: M.H. Gutiérrez

steps.title: Collect samples in sterile 50 mL...
steps.instruction: Collect samples in sterile 50 mL polypropylene tubes and fix with formaldehyde or glutaraldehyde (2% final concentration). Store samples at 4 °C in the dark.

steps.title: Stain filters with the retained material...
steps.instruction: Stain filters with the retained material from filtration by applying 600 µL of aqueous 0.1% Calcofluor White directly to sample and filter, making sure to cover the entire area of the filter. Allow 00:05:00 to 00:10:00 then use vacuum to remove the excess stain from the filter. Avoid any light source.

steps.title: Filter 5 mL to 30 mL...
steps.instruction: Filter 5 mL to 30 mL (depending on the environment) of seawater by 0.22-µm mesh black 25 mm diameter polycarbonate filters (Millipore Corp.).

steps.title: Place the filter (sample side up)...
steps.instruction: Place the filter (sample side up) onto a slide and add 1 drop of non-fluorescent immersion oil to the top of the filter, then cover with a cover slip.

steps.title: Count slides immediately on epifluorescent microscope...
steps.instruction: Count slides immediately on epifluorescent microscope or store at -20 °C .

steps.title: Use an epifluorescence microscope equipped with...
steps.instruction: Use an epifluorescence microscope equipped with UV filter (used for DAPI, eg. filter set 49 Carl Zeiss Ltd., 365 nm excitation and 445-450 nm emission band pass) to examine at 1000X the entire effective area of the filters.

steps.title: Count all hyphae identified and record their length and width
steps.instruction: Count all hyphae identified and record their length and width. Use cylinder volume as a morphological approximation to estimate the biovolume of fungal filaments. Biomass can be estimated based from biomass: biovolume ratios described from fungi (Newell and Statzell-Tallman, 1982).
